expectations.text: Lists DB_HOST environment variable for database host
expectations.evidence: Section 'Database Configuration' lists 'DB_HOST' with Purpose: 'PostgreSQL database host address', Type: 'String (hostname or IP address)', Example: 'postgres.example.com or 10.0.1.5'

expectations.text: Lists DB_PORT environment variable for database port
expectations.evidence: Section 'Database Configuration' lists 'DB_PORT' with Purpose: 'PostgreSQL database port number', Type: 'Integer', Example: '5432 (PostgreSQL default)'

expectations.text: Lists DB_NAME environment variable for database name
expectations.evidence: Section 'Database Configuration' lists 'DB_NAME' with Purpose: 'Name of the PostgreSQL database to connect to', Type: 'String', Example: 'shopdb_prod or onlineshop_production'

expectations.text: Lists DB_USERNAME environment variable for database username
expectations.evidence: Section 'Database Configuration' lists 'DB_USERNAME' with Purpose: 'PostgreSQL database username for authentication', Type: 'String', Example: 'shopuser_prod or app_user'

expectations.text: Lists DB_PASSWORD environment variable for database password
expectations.evidence: Section 'Database Configuration' lists 'DB_PASSWORD' with Purpose: 'PostgreSQL database password for authentication', Type: 'String (sensitive)', Example: 'A strong password like xK9!mP2$vN8@qR5#'

expectations.text: Lists JWT_SECRET environment variable for token signing
expectations.evidence: Section 'Security Configuration' lists 'JWT_SECRET' with Purpose: 'Base64-encoded secret key used to sign and validate JWT authentication tokens', Type: 'String (Base64-encoded, sensitive)'
Section 'CORS Configuration' lists 'CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS' with Purpose: 'Comma-separated list of frontend URLs allowed to make cross-origin requests to the API', Type: 'String (comma-separated list)'

expectations.text: Explains what each environment variable is used for
expectations.evidence: Each variable has a dedicated subsection with 'Purpose', 'Used in', and detailed explanations. For example, DB_HOST explains it's 'Used in: spring.datasource.url=jdbc:postgresql://${DB_HOST}:${DB_PORT}/${DB_NAME}' and JWT_SECRET explains 'This is the MOST CRITICAL security variable' with usage context
JWT_SECRET section explicitly states 'Type: String (Base64-encoded, sensitive)' and provides generation instruction: 'Generate using: openssl rand -base64 32'

claims.claim: JWT_SECRET must be at least 256 bits (32 bytes) for HMAC-SHA256 security
claims.evidence: Document states 'Must be at least 256 bits (32 bytes) for HMAC-SHA256 security' and provides generation command 'openssl rand -base64 32' which generates 32 bytes

claims.claim: CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS should not use wildcards (*) in production
claims.evidence: Important section states 'Do NOT use wildcards (*) in production for security reasons' and 'Should only include ALL frontend domains that will access the API'

claims.claim: Default token lifetime is 86400000 milliseconds (24 hours)
claims.evidence: JWT_SECRET section notes 'Token Lifetime: Configured in app.jwt.expiration=86400000 (24 hours in milliseconds)'

claims.claim: All 7 environment variables are required for production deployment
claims.evidence: Complete Environment Variables Checklist section lists export commands for all 7 variables with comment '# For production deployment, ensure ALL of these are set'

eval_feedback.overall: Excellent comprehensive assertions. All critical environment variables are checked, and the assertion requiring explanations ensures the output provides value beyond just listing names. The Base64 encoding detail is a discriminating test that shows technical depth.
